speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Monday, October 9, 1995. Survivor, Charlene Schiff. The interviewer, Ellen Baskin. City, state, Los Angeles, California, United States of America. And the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 9, 1995. I'm going to be interviewing Charlene Schiff. My name is Ellen Baskin. We're in Woodland Hills, California in the United States of America, and the language will be English. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Charlene Schiff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, my name was Shulamit Perlmutter, and they used to call me Musia Perlmutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Shulamit for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. S-H-U-L-A-M-I-T. Some people add an H, but I do not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Perlmutter, could you spell--

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-R-L-M-U-T-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your nickname mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Musia, it was a derivative. It's Polish, Jewish. It's M-U-S-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born December 16, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in eastern Poland. And the name of the town is Horochow. And to spell Horochow is H-O-R-O-C-H-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family consisted of two wonderful parents and an older sister who was four years my senior. My father's name was Simcha Perlmutter, or Simon Perlmutter. My mother's name was Fruma Perlmlutter, or Freida Perlmutter. My sister's name was Tchyja. It's a Hebrew name, and it's spelled T-C-H-Y-J-A. But she also had a nickname. They used to call her Tia, T-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the town living like at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our lives in my town, Horochow, at that time was idyllic, as far as I can recall. We had a home that was open for the community in Horochow. Both of my parents were civic leaders, and they were involved in Jewish causes, as well as community causes. My father was a professor at the University of Lviv, which is about an hour and a half from Horochow. And my mother, who also was a teacher, was doing philanthropic work. I do not recall her teaching in Horochow. My sister and I were going to school. My father was emphasizing education. And consequently, both my sister and I had private tutors from the time we were four years of age. Our home was a home filled with love, hospitality to everyone, and culture. I recall we used to have recitals, poetry readings, book reports, all sorts of cultural activities in our home. To these activities, or to these happenings, the entire town was involved and invited. There were not just Jewish representatives, but also the starosta, which is the equivalent to a mayor in the city; and the clergy, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the equivalent to a priest, and the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the Ukrainian priest. And so the whole community was like one big family. I remember my mother organized camps for poor children in the summertime. And these camps were for all poor children in town, not just the Jewish children. And I would like to stress this very strongly, that my parents were both involved in all sorts of community activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father taught philosophy. And someone asked me the other day if it was mathematical philosophy. I didn't know. And I don't know. But I know it was philosophy, yes. And I also recall that there were many times when he had meetings with his students who came. And he would go into a room, and we were not invited to these meetings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have friends in town? What did you do as a small child for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had our neighbors. We had friends from school. And we led a very active life. And from what I recall, it was a very good life. And also I would like to mention I did not sense any anti-Semitism as a child. I'm sure there was anti-Semitism, but I did not sense it. As a matter of fact, in retrospect, I realize now that my father's higher education was obtained in different parts of Europe, not in Poland. And the reason being is as a Jew, he was not allowed to enter the universities. I guess there was the 'numerus clausus,' which means a quota for Jews. And his advanced degrees and doctorates were from various parts. I think one was from Switzerland. He also attended the Sorbonne and some place in Germany. I'm not sure if it was Heidelberg or somewhere else. But that I did not realize at the time. I would also like to mention that my father was a visiting professor at the University in Krakow. And this is the Jagellonska Universitetet, well known throughout the world. And that was-- I think the year that they started this university was the 15th century. I can't recall the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go with your father to either of these universities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We used to go to Lviv, which was a treat for my sister and me. And we never went together. My father-- this was time for us one on one. And now and then our father would take us to Lviv, to the university. And it was a fantastic treat. As a matter of fact, in 1988 when we returned to my hometown, our son went with us to the university. And it was very poignant because I did meet an elderly gentleman, who was probably a janitor, who told me that he remembered my father. I don't know if it's true, but it gave my son a real lift and a connection with my past.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the visits to the universities?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember in particular one big building, which still stands, because Lviv was not damaged very much during the war. And I remember it was-- to use the terminology now-- awesome. But it was very good. My father was a very tall person. And it was very uplifting to be with him in the halls of higher learning. And he always tried to speak on a level so that we could understand him. But it was always a little bit above. And I remember the train ride to get there, or sometimes by car. We did not own a car, but some of the other professors did. And it was a very nice time because in those days when I went with him, I had him all to myself. And you know, again, chronologically I'm digressing a little bit. But our home was a very loving home, but it was also a very disciplined home. And when we sat down to dinner, we did not speak until father spoke first. He was served first. He had a private library with-- my father collected rare editions, first editions and rare books. And he was quite known in the entire area. And somehow people would come when they would have these rare books, and he would pay through the nose to obtain these very valuable books. But we were not allowed into the library by ourselves. We had to wash our hands, and he would take out some books, or something for us to look at, but not without his permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a town was Horochow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure how big a town it was. But I know that there were approximately 5,000 Jews, approximately. And as far as I know, there are several people who went to Palestine at that time, or escaped through Russia at the time. But I know only of one other survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question to answer. We were observant as far as tradition goes, but I don't think we attended synagogue religiously every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Sabbath meal every week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, we did. And my mother and father entertained very frequently. And our home was sort of like an open house for everyone. And there were always people, and it was a very happy home. And I think that that background fortified me for later on, because I felt very cared for and very loved. And it gave me security at the time. And I know people say now that in retrospect, people who survived, they idealize their past. I don't think I do. And again, I feel that my parents were perfect for their time. And they gave me-- they instilled in me very right values of life. And I am trying, in turn, to do the same thing with our son. I don't know if I'm very successful, but I'm trying. And somehow these values have not changed to this day. I mean, it's still to do unto others, and to believe in a higher power, and to be good to everyone. And that is still the value today, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your parents ever talking about things that were going on in the larger world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. I know my father had five sisters and a mother, my grandmother, who ended up in the United States. And my father chronologically was somewhere in between his five sisters. I'm not quite sure, I think somewhere in the middle. But being the only son, he was the last one to emigrate. And unfortunately, the war interrupted that. I do recall that we ended up in Warsaw and in Lviv being interviewed at the consulate. And the papers were being prepared for our immigration to America. And then the war started, and that put an end to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he want to go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because his entire family, immediate family, was already in America, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had gone before the rise of Nazism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. I don't know when exactly. And now I only have one living aunt, and she's a little hazy on that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Bertha Lieberman. And when I first came to the United States, she is the one who took me to Columbus, Ohio-- that's where her home was-- and threw me, literally, into school. I came on a Friday. I think it was Friday. And on Monday, I went to Central High School without knowing a word of English. And that idea did not go over very well with me, but I'm glad she did what she did, because that was the right thing to do, is to get an education for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, going back, though--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. That's-- to the time before the war, do you remember any sense of the conversations changing, or the behavior of townspeople changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I do know, ironically enough, that my father was given two tickets to the World's Fair in 1939 in New York from the university. And he refused the two tickets. He asked for four. And they didn't give him four, and someone else got the two tickets. It was very sad. And other than that, I don't know. I know there were some meetings that my father had with some of his students, and that was behind closed doors. And later, my husband and I visited Israel. And some of my father's students came, and we had a very nice reunion. And apparently what was happening, my father was helping them get out of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your recollections of the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't have many severe recollections. I remember that when the war started, September 1, 1939, there were airplanes flying overhead. And all of a sudden, there were these long columns of-- they didn't call them refugees then. But these were people who were trying to come from the western part of Poland to go I don't know where. But there were lots of people walking somewhere, going through the town, going further and further into, apparently, Russia. And the Soviet Union occupied our little town without much-- without any bloodshed. And at that time my life, our life, our family life, did not change much, because my father still had the job. I mean, he kept his university job. And the only thing that changed is the language. It used to be Polish, and now it became Russian. And that was not much of a problem because most Europeans are bi- or trilingual out of necessity because our neighbors speak different languages. And consequently, we have to learn, then. So that's the only thing that happened. Obviously there were many changes, but I did not realize them. There were changes. For instance, most of the business people in town, if they had businesses-- I mean the ones who had businesses before the war-- they were nationalized. In the beginning, everything was plentiful. But eventually, I mean, they ran out of certain clothing. Shoes were hard to get. Other than that, I don't know. But this is all in retrospect. At the time when it occurred, I did not realize any of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to continue to go to school in the early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I was. And so was my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to a school with both Jews and Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a public school. And so did my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any time in those years did you begin to sense a change in the attitude towards Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not. I still had Christian friends and Jewish friends. And this is-- I don't know. Maybe I was old enough to realize it, but I didn't. And that's why it's so very hard for me and very sad to acknowledge what happened afterwards. Because everything that happened, nobody extended a helping hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the bad things start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were under the Soviet occupation from '39 to '41, summer. And then, at that time, when the Germans came in, in the middle-- I don't recall exactly the dates. In early summer, the Germans came in without fanfare, without bloodshed. The local populace greeted them with flowers and very happy faces. I guess most of the adults realized what was happening, because I recall that my mother took all the little jewelry-- we were not rich in a sense of having much money and all, because my parents were intellectuals. But whatever jewelry she had, she divided it and she sewed it into our clothing, outer clothing. And when I asked, why are you doing that, she says, that's for a rainy day. But right when the Germans came in, within two or three days they came around with the Ukrainians from our town, with a list of about 300 Jewish leaders, and my father among them. They grabbed my father, didn't even give him a chance to put on a jacket. And that was the last time I saw my father. I tried to find out what happened, but I was never successful in definitely establishing what happened to my father. After that, for the next two weeks they rounded up the Jews in the marketplace. And we had to watch when they burned our synagogue and all the other little-- we had one main synagogue and several smaller ones. And the Germans burned the synagogues. They took out all the Torahs and all the books of prayer, and they burned that. And we had to watch. That was the beginning. After that, every day they came up with new decrees. First, we needed to bring all the silver, gold, all the price-- all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Valuable.

speaker: interviewee
text: --valuables to the marketplace. And this was going on every day for about two weeks. We were ordered to wear a yellow star on the front of our clothing and on the back. Everyone over 14 years of age was ordered to slave labor. I was not 14, so I was left to myself. My mother was digging ditches, and so was my sister, and building roads, supposedly. And many a day, my mother and sister would come home black and blue from the beatings that they received because they didn't please, I guess, the people who were watching them do their work. After about two, three weeks, before everyone reported for work, we were told to gather in the marketplace again and to take with us only what we could carry. That's it. And then they herded us into the poorest section of town. And this is the way the ghetto was formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I took a small suitcase with some of my clothing and the little coat-- it was a short coat where my mother sewed in the jewelry. And I think I took some crayons. Don't remember exactly what else. I did not take any toys at all. I know my mother took some pillows and blankets, and she took the jacket that my father never got to wear. We ended up in the ghetto, which was enclosed on all sides with a wooden fence reinforced with barbed wire. And there were only two gates, and you had to have a permit to enter and a permit to leave. We were assigned a room with three other families. And that was a big house. I think it was a three-story house. The house, I think, housed at that time about 100 people. We had one bathroom and one primitive kitchen for the entire house. In our room with three other families, there wasn't enough space on the floor for all of us to sleep. And so we had to make, like, bunk beds to accommodate everyone. In the beginning, life in ghetto was very chaotic. People were disoriented. And I think the goal of the Germans, to rob us of all the dignity that was left at the time, did-- the Germans accomplished it. We didn't have any connection with the outside world. There was no mail. There were no newspapers. There were no radios. There was nothing. Just we were left to our own devices.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was a little over 10, I think. Yeah, a little over 10 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was about 1941?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. How could I have been 12?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were born in '29.

speaker: interviewee
text: December '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about. Yeah, not quite. All right. My arithmetic is not very good. We stayed in the ghetto. And everyone who worked, over 14 years of age, received a very small ration. Under 14 years of age, one didn't work, and consequently didn't get any food at all. So my mother and my sister shared their small rations with me. Children like myself were left during the day with nothing to do. There was a whole group of children. We got together and we dug a hole. I call it a tunnel, but it was not a tunnel. It was a hole. And we made a way of getting out of the ghetto. Yes. I'm proud to say that none of the children ever gave hole away or denounced it. We would sneak out in the middle of the day and remove our yellow stars, and try to either beg or buy some kind of food. I want to trace back a little bit. In the beginning of the ghetto, my mother and several other women were also able-- or they established a clandestine school for children like myself. And that school existed for several months in the evenings after the women came back from work. And what they did, they bartered on the outside, and they were able to obtain some paper, and some crayons, and some books. And we really looked forward to this school because it gave us something that a normal child would have. And it took our thoughts away from the hunger, and from the drudgery, and hopelessness of it all. But that school existed only for several months because pretty soon, they ran out of money to barter. And also, there was such a hopelessness, and dismay, and chaos, and confusion. And everybody became very much preoccupied with just trying to survive somehow. And so the school disappeared. And now coming back to our so-called tunnel, we did go out-- and much to my mother's chagrin, even though I promised her I would not do it. Because if one was caught outside of the ghetto, one paid with one's life. But hunger is a very strong motivator. And we did go out and try to obtain some food. I remember one incident where I did get out and I sold a small ruby-- a gold and ruby ring for two eggs. And I was wearing a dress with puffed sleeves, and I put an egg in each puff. And unfortunately, one of the Ukrainian guards caught me. And he found the eggs. He threw the eggs on the sidewalk and made me kneel down and rub my face in the egg mixture, and screamed for me to get back where I belong and never to come out again outside. He was one of the kind-hearted ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to break to change tapes. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
